section.title: Sing Aloud to God Our Strength
section.sectionRationale: An Asaphite festival psalm that opens in full-voiced praise at a feast, then shifts into God's own first-person address, recalling the Exodus and Meribah, warning against idolatry, and lamenting Israel's refusal to listen, before closing with a wistful promise of finest wheat and honey from the rock.
section.thesis: God still longs to fill the open mouths of His people with the finest provision, but stubborn hearts that will not listen forfeit the very blessings and victories God is eager to give.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the joyful call to feast, 1-3; the festival's memorial of deliverance, 4-7; the solemn warning against idolatry, 8-10; and the sorrow of disobedience answered by the longing promise of blessing, 11-16.

verses.text: Sing for joy to God our strength; make a joyful noise to the God of Jacob.
Lift up a song, strike the tambourine, play the sweet-sounding harp and lyre.
Sound the ram's horn at the New Moon, and at the full moon on the day of our Feast.
For this is a statute for Israel, an ordinance of the God of Jacob.
He ordained it as a testimony for Joseph when he went out over the land of Egypt, where I heard an unfamiliar language:
"I relieved his shoulder of the burden; his hands were freed from the basket.
You called out in distress, and I rescued you; I answered you from the cloud of thunder; I {{nasah|tested}} you at the waters of Meribah. Selah
{{shama|Hear}}, O My people, and I will warn you: O Israel, if only you would listen to Me!
There must be no strange god among you, nor shall you bow to a foreign god.
I am the LORD your God, who brought you up out of Egypt. Open wide your mouth, and I will fill it.

units.label: The sorrow of disobedience, the promise of blessing
units.note: if only, and yet I would

verses.text: But My people would not listen to Me, and Israel would not obey Me.
So I gave them up to their stubborn hearts to follow their own devices.
If only My people would listen to Me, if Israel would follow My ways,
how soon I would subdue their enemies and turn My hand against their foes!
Those who hate the LORD would feign obedience, and their doom would last forever.
But I would feed you the finest wheat; with {{devash|honey}} from the rock I would satisfy you."

shama.gloss: To hear, listen, and obey, a single word covering both attention and response. God's repeated 'if only you would listen' (shama) treats hearing and obeying as one act, not two separate steps.

nasah.gloss: To test or try. At Meribah God tested Israel's trust; the same root elsewhere describes Israel testing God, showing the wilderness as a place where faith or its absence was proven on both sides.

devash.gloss: Honey. God's promised 'honey from the rock' pictures unexpected sweetness supplied from the hardest, driest places, a foretaste of the abundant provision and living water Christ offers, John 4:14, 6:35.

basic.context: This festival psalm opens with full-throated praise at a feast, most likely the Feast of Trumpets or a similar new moon celebration, then shifts to God speaking directly, recalling how He rescued Israel from Egypt and tested them at Meribah. God pleads with His people to listen and forsake other gods, laments that they would not, and closes wistfully with the blessing He still longs to give.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm moves from celebration to divine oracle to lament, showing worship as the natural setting for hearing God's own voice recall His deeds and press His claims. The repeated 'if only' expresses genuine divine longing for covenant obedience, not resignation.
basic.originalLanguages: God's plea, 'if only you would listen,' uses shama, a word that binds hearing and obeying together as a single act. He recalls testing them, nasah, at Meribah, and promises devash, honey, from the rock, sweetness drawn from the hardest ground, if only they would listen.
basic.typology: The 'finest wheat' and 'honey from the rock' God still longs to give anticipate the far greater provision Christ offers, the bread of life and living water that truly satisfy, John 6:35, 4:14. Israel's testing at Meribah becomes the very episode Hebrews uses to warn believers not to harden their hearts.
basic.christ: Jesus offers Himself as the bread of life, John 6:35, and the living water, John 4:14, the true and final fulfillment of the finest wheat and honey from the rock this psalm holds out. Hebrews 3:7-19 applies the Meribah testing directly as a warning and a call to faith in Christ today.
basic.god: God is shown pleading, not merely commanding, longing for His people's attention and trust, quick to recall His past rescue, painfully honest about giving stubborn hearts over to their own devices, yet still eager, even after refusal, to satisfy His people abundantly.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 3:7-19 cites the Meribah testing as a direct warning to trust God today rather than harden the heart. John 6:35 shows Jesus as the bread that truly satisfies, beyond even the finest wheat. Romans 1:24-28 echoes the pattern of God giving people over to their own stubborn hearts.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's ache, God longing to fill wide-open mouths with finest wheat and honey from the rock, while His people refuse to listen, finds its resolution in Christ, the true bread and living water who satisfies all who will finally listen and come to Him.

context.literary: A festival psalm in two voices, first the congregation's call to worship, 1-3, then God's own first-person oracle, 4-16, moving from Exodus memory to warning to lament to a wistful, unfulfilled promise of blessing.
context.historical: Likely sung at a major feast, possibly the Feast of Trumpets or the full-moon feast of Booths or Passover, in a period, perhaps under Hezekiah or Josiah, when leaders called Israel back to covenant faithfulness and away from idolatry.
context.canonical: Part of the Asaphite collection's concern for covenant loyalty, and directly cited by Hebrews as a warning against hardening the heart the way Israel did at Meribah, Hebrews 3:7-4:11.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call God's people to full-voiced festival worship that remembers His past deliverance, to press home His exclusive claim against every idol, and to voice His genuine longing for obedience that His people, in fact, refused to give.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical pattern of festival worship and covenant unfaithfulness; it prescribes an abiding pattern of grateful, attentive worship and undivided loyalty to the LORD as the condition for experiencing His fullest blessing.

debates.question: Does 'I gave them up to their stubborn hearts,' verse 12, describe a one-time historical judgment or an ongoing principle of how God deals with persistent refusal?

views.label: A specific historical description of God's response to Israel's wilderness and later unfaithfulness, judged by withholding further intervention at that time
views.case: The immediate context recalls specific past events, Egypt and Meribah, and describes God's response within that history rather than stating a timeless formula.

views.label: A recurring principle of divine judgment, seen again in Romans 1:24-28, where persistent refusal to listen results in God giving people over to their own chosen path
views.case: Paul's near-identical language in Romans 1 suggests the psalm states a pattern true of God's dealings with hardened hearts in any era, not only ancient Israel's.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree this is a real and serious act of divine judgment, and both call hearers to listen and obey now rather than presume on future opportunity.

study.originalLanguages: God's repeated plea uses shama, hear and obey together as a single response, not mere passive listening. He recalls nasah, testing His people at Meribah, and closes with the promise of devash, honey, from the rock, sweetness from the hardest place, held out to those who would only listen.

typology.type: Finest wheat and honey from the rock, verse 16
typology.fulfillment: Christ as the true bread of life and living water that fully satisfy, John 6:35, John 4:14.

typology.type: The testing at the waters of Meribah, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: Cited directly in Hebrews 3:7-19 as a warning to believers not to harden their hearts against Christ today.

study.christ: This psalm's unfulfilled longing, 'open wide your mouth, and I will fill it,' finds its true answer in Jesus, who declares Himself the bread of life, John 6:35, and offers living water that satisfies forever, John 4:14. The Meribah testing this psalm recalls, verse 7, becomes in Hebrews 3:7-19 a direct call to trust Christ today rather than repeat Israel's hardened refusal to listen.
study.god: God is shown as a pleading, longing covenant partner, recalling His own past rescue in tender detail, honest about the real judgment of giving stubborn hearts over to their own devices, yet still holding out, even in lament, the finest blessing He is eager to give to those who will listen.

crossReferences.note: Applies the Meribah testing of this psalm directly as a warning against hardening the heart against Christ.
Jesus as the bread of life fulfills the psalm's promise of the finest wheat for those who listen.
Echoes the pattern of God giving persistently unresponsive hearts over to their own devices.

study.oneStory: Psalm 81 voices God's own ache over a people who would not listen, even though He longed to fill their open mouths with the finest wheat and honey from the rock. That longing is answered in Jesus, the true bread and living water, who satisfies fully everyone who will finally listen and come.

sermon.title: Honey from the Rock
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly from Psalm 81, showing how its wilderness imagery, the rock and the honey, points to Jesus, who was tested in the wilderness on our behalf and now satisfies His people through that finished work.

quiz.q: How does the psalm call the congregation to worship, verses 1-3?

options.text: With joyful singing, tambourine, harp, lyre, and the ram's horn at the festival.
options.note: Verses 1-3 list exactly this full-voiced, instrument-filled celebration.

options.text: With complete silence and no instruments.
options.note: The opposite is described; the psalm calls for joyful, loud celebration.

options.text: With mourning rites appropriate to a funeral.
options.note: This is a joyful festival call, not a funeral lament.

options.text: With private, individual prayer only.
options.note: The imagery is thoroughly communal and festive, not solitary.

quiz.q: What does God recall doing for Israel in verses 6-7?

options.text: Relieving their burden in Egypt and rescuing them when they called out in distress.
options.note: Verses 6-7 describe exactly this deliverance.

options.text: Punishing them for leaving Egypt.
options.note: God recalls rescuing them, not punishing their departure.

options.text: Ignoring their cries for help entirely.
options.note: The text says God answered them from the cloud of thunder, not that He ignored them.

options.text: Sending them back into slavery.
options.note: God's action described here is deliverance from slavery, not a return to it.

quiz.q: What warning does God give in verse 9?

options.text: There must be no strange god among them, nor should they bow to a foreign god.
options.note: Verse 9 states this warning directly.

options.text: They must build more temples to various gods.
options.note: The warning is against idolatry, the opposite of building more idol shrines.

options.text: They should stop worshiping altogether.
options.note: The concern is exclusive worship of the LORD, not the end of worship.

options.text: They should worship foreign gods alongside the LORD.
options.note: The verse explicitly forbids any foreign god, not a shared worship arrangement.

quiz.q: How did Israel actually respond to God's plea to listen, verse 11?

options.text: My people would not listen to Me, and Israel would not obey Me.
options.note: Verse 11 states this refusal directly.

options.text: They listened perfectly and obeyed every command.
options.note: The text states the opposite, a refusal to listen and obey.

options.text: They listened but forgot within a single day.
options.note: The text describes an outright refusal to listen, not a brief lapse.

options.text: They listened only when it was convenient for them.
options.note: The verse states a general refusal, not selective or conditional listening.

quiz.q: What is God's longed-for promise in verse 16, if His people would only listen?

options.text: He would feed them the finest wheat and satisfy them with honey from the rock.
options.note: This is the exact promise of verse 16, fulfilled ultimately in Christ.

options.text: He would remove all festivals and celebrations.
options.note: The promise is abundant provision, not the removal of worship.

options.text: He would give them over permanently to their enemies.
options.note: This is the opposite of the promised blessing; that fate belongs to those who hate the LORD, verse 15.

options.text: He would delay any blessing indefinitely.
options.note: The promise is immediate and abundant, 'how soon,' verse 14, not indefinitely delayed.

questions: The psalm opens in full-voiced festival praise, verses 1-3. What would it look like for your own worship, whether alone or with others, to be marked by that same wholehearted joy?
God recalls relieving Israel's burden and rescuing them when they cried out, verses 6-7. What burden has God already relieved in your life that you need to remember today?
God pleads, 'if only you would listen to Me,' verses 8, 13. Where is God currently asking for your attention and obedience, and what is keeping you from listening fully?
Verse 12 shows the real cost of persistent refusal, being given over to one's own stubborn heart. Where might you be experiencing the painful freedom of getting exactly what you insisted on?

God still longs to fill wide-open mouths with the finest wheat and honey from the rock, verse 16, fulfilled in Christ, the bread of life. What would it mean this week to come to Jesus with your mouth, and your life, wide open?

translationNotes.note: 'Gittith,' in the psalm's heading, likely names a melody or instrument, possibly brought from the Philistine city of Gath; it appears also in the headings of Psalms 8 and 84.
'An unfamiliar language' recalls Israel's time in Egypt among a foreign tongue, underscoring how far God reached to rescue His people from genuine bondage.
'Meribah' means quarreling or strife, naming the place, Exodus 17:7, where Israel tested God over water, an episode Hebrews 3 later uses as a direct warning to believers.

Footnote reference markers in verses 5 and 7 removed; no em/en dashes were present in the verse text. Verse count (16) and first/last verse wording checked against the section map. Sermon link verified this session: Timothy Keller, 'Honey from the Rock' (podcast.gospelinlife.com), preached on Psalm 81 at Redeemer Presbyterian Church.
